coredata.dc:title: Design and photophysical insights on graphene quantum dots for use as nanosensor in differentiating methamphetamine and morphine in solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Masteri-Farahani, Majid
Askari, Faezeh
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared and utilized as nanosensor for differentiation and determination of two most common narcotic drugs i.e. morphine and methamphetamine. The microstructure and optical properties of the GQDs were investigated by various physicochemical methods. XRD analysis indicated low crystalline nature, demonstrating the graphitic nature of the GQDs. According to the Tauc plot derived from UV–Vis spectrum, the optical band gap of the GQDs was determined to ~4.98 eV, assigned to the n–π* transitions. Cyclic voltammetry analysis of the GQDs determined electrochemical band gap of ~4.88 eV with HOMO and LUMO energies equal to −6.83 eV and −1.95 eV, respectively. The GQDs were employed as fluorescent sensing probe for determination of morphine and methamphetamine. The blue fluorescence of the prepared GQDs under the excitation at 362 nm was quenched in the presence of methamphetamine and enhanced in the presence of morphine. The detection limits of 1.48 and 0.5 μg/ml were found for methamphetamine and morphine, respectively. This inexpensive sensing system shows some advantages such as short response time (t < 1 min) and low detection limit as well as nontoxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Band gap
Morphine
Methamphetamine
Sensor

Analysis of the Fluorescence Response of the GQDs to the Methamphetamine and Morphine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of Kharazmi University . (Grant Number D/2061 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structures of l‑morphine and d‑methamphetamine.

(a) EDX spectrum and (b) XRD pattern of the GQDs, (c) FT-IR spectra of citric acid and GQDs, (d) AFM image of GQDs on mica substrates; Inset: (up) The height profile along the line drawn in the AFM image and (bottom) the AFM image of mica surface.
UV–Vis spectrum and Tauc plot (inset plot) of the GQDs.
Cyclic voltammograms of the GQDs/glassy carbon and bare glassy carbon electrodes for determination of HOMO and LUMO energy levels.
HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the GQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of the GQDs (excitation wavelength = 360 nm) (Inset: Photographs of the GQDs solution under visible and UV light), (b) excitation wavelength dependence of fluorescence spectra of the GQDs.
pH-dependent UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (Inset: Photographs of the GQDs solutions in different pH conditions under UV light).

UV–Vis absorption (dash) and fluorescence (line) spectra of GQDs solution by gradual addition of methamphetamine (top) and morphine (bottom). Insets: linear relationship between the FL intensity of the GQDs and concentration of methamphetamine and morphine. F0 and F are the FL intensities in the absence and presence of narcotic drugs, respectively; the inset of the bottom indicates the brighter blue emission of GQDs by addition of morphine under UV light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Time-dependent fluorescence response of the GQDs to methamphetamine (left) and morphine (right).
Elemental analysis results of citric acid and GQDs.
Comparison of the present approach with other reported methods for the detection of drugs.

head.ce:title: Design and photophysical insights on graphene quantum dots for use as nanosensor in differentiating methamphetamine and morphine in solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Islamic Republic of Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared and utilized as nanosensor for differentiation and determination of two most common narcotic drugs i.e. morphine and methamphetamine. The microstructure and optical properties of the GQDs were investigated by various physicochemical methods. XRD analysis indicated low crystalline nature, demonstrating the graphitic nature of the GQDs. According to the Tauc plot derived from UV–Vis spectrum, the optical band gap of the GQDs was determined to ~4.98 eV, assigned to the n–π* transitions. Cyclic voltammetry analysis of the GQDs determined electrochemical band gap of ~4.88 eV with HOMO and LUMO energies equal to −6.83 eV and −1.95 eV, respectively. The GQDs were employed as fluorescent sensing probe for determination of morphine and methamphetamine. The blue fluorescence of the prepared GQDs under the excitation at 362 nm was quenched in the presence of methamphetamine and enhanced in the presence of morphine. The detection limits of 1.48 and 0.5 μg/ml were found for methamphetamine and morphine, respectively. This inexpensive sensing system shows some advantages such as short response time (t < 1 min) and low detection limit as well as nontoxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as new carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted great attention owing to their exceptional properties and advantages. Some properties such as low toxicity, fluorescence properties, chemical inertness, excellent photostability biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence properties, and facile functionalization with biomolecules have awarded them several biological and chemical applications [1–3].

Narcotic drugs such as morphine and methamphetamine are used in large scale and the addiction is global problem. Thus, their detection and determination are very essential to control their abuse and several analyses have been used for the determination of these narcotics such as thin-layer chromatography (TLC), gas chromatography (GC), immunoassay methods, and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4–8]. In spite of the accuracy of such techniques, their several preparation steps make them difficult and time-consuming methods [9,10]. Moreover, these procedures require access to relatively expensive instruments which is not available easily for the continuous and quick analyses of suspicious samples. Thus, several attempts have been made to achieve easy, accurate, and cost-effective analytic methods to solve these problems [11,12].

Quantum dots (QDs) with core-shell or alloyed structures render appropriate photophysical properties for several applications based on energy transfer phenomena such as sensitive detection and measurement of various analytes as well as light harvesting devices [13–17]. In comparison with common organic fluorophores, QDs reveal many remarkable characteristics such as size-tunability and efficient light absorption over a wide range of wavelengths [18–20]. However, their toxicity and poor solubility limited their wide utilizations especially in biological systems. In recent years, GQDs appeared as excellent fluorophores for bioimaging and optical sensing due to their distinctive advantages [21]. Due to their surface functionalities, GQDs and their derivatives have been used in highly sensitive detection and determination of various target species such as metal ions [22,23], chlorine [24], H2O2 [25], glucose [26,27], dopamine [28], melamine [29], several amino acids and proteins [30–33].

Here, we introduce a sensitive, rapid, facile, and especially green nanosensor for detection and determination of two important narcotic drugs i.e. d-methamphetamine and l-morphine (Fig. 1 ). To our knowledge, this is the first fluorescence study of interaction between GQDs and narcotic drugs. For this purpose, GQDs were prepared with simple and green procedure and then characterized with various physicochemical methods. Due to the existence of superficial functional groups, GQDs can interact with morphine and methamphetamine drugs in the excited state. Surprisingly, the interaction of these drugs with the surface groups of the GQDs results in opposite changes in fluorescence intensity of GQDs. Hence, the GQDs based nanosensor provide advantages over the classic methods of analysis as no elaborated sample preparation is required and the analysis is considerably time-saving. Furthermore, this method may be developed into portable optical sensors and simple qualitative or quantitative diagnostic kits.
Details of used materials and equipment have been included in supporting information.

The GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid according to literature method with some modification [34]. Briefly, 1 g of citric acid was heated to 200 °C in a 50 ml beaker under static air. The citric acid was melted and a yellowish color was appeared after some minutes. With further heating, the color turned to orange indicating the formation of GQDs. Various reaction times from 10 to 40 min were examined and finally the reaction time of 15 min was chosen to prepare the GQDs with the best fluorescence spectrum. Further heating times was avoided as results in the formation of dark graphene oxide. After cooling the obtained GQD solution to ambient temperature, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 with 0.1 M NaOH. After evaporation of the solution, the solid GQDs were achieved. 10 mg of the GQDs was dissolved in phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 10 ml, pH 7.4) for following emission studies.

Stock aqueous solutions of l-morphine hydrochloride (0.66 mM) and d-methamphetamine hydrochloride (2.7 mM) were prepared. 3 ml of the prepared GQDs solution (1 mg·ml−1 in PBS) was poured into a quartz cuvette. Then, given volumes of the aqueous solution of l-morphine or d-methamphetamine were added and the fluorescence spectra of GQDs solutions were recorded after each addition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs From Citric Acid Precursor
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid as starting material can readily give GQDs through the pyrolysis and intermolecular dehydration of hydroxyl groups. The synthetic mechanism is similar to the other reported procedure starting from glucose as precursor [35]. The chemical composition, degree of condensation, and physicochemical properties of the resulting GQDs can be tuned by controlling the pyrolysis conditions [36]. The chemical composition of the citric acid and obtained GQDs was determined by energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy and CHN elemental analysis and the results are given in Fig. 2a and Table 1 . The results show more carbon and less hydrogen and oxygen percentages of GQDs in comparison with citric acid precursor, indicating the carbonization of citric acid and formation of GQDs.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of prepared GQDs exhibits a broad diffraction peak centered at about 2θ = 25° (Fig. 2b) indicating the relatively disordered assembling and the absence of short range order due to the presence of several oxygen containing functional groups in this material. Also, with using the Bragg equation, the interlayer spacing was calculated to be about 0.36 nm indicating the formation of long range ordered graphitic structure. The position of observed peak and the interlayer spacing of graphene sheets are in accordance with earlier reports on the successful preparation of GQDs [37].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was utilized to explore the functional groups located on the obtained GQDs and provide further evidence for the conversion of citric acid to GQDs (Fig. 2c). The sharpness of peaks in FT-IR spectrum of citric acid arises from its crystalline nature. Both the GQDs and citric acid show the characteristic stretching vibrations of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. The stretching vibrations of CH bonds is observed in GQDs (~2970 cm−1) indicating that the graphitization is not complete and there are some aliphatic CH bonds on the edges of the obtained GQDs. Conclusively, the similarity of the peaks in both GQDs and citric acid indicates that in spite of the change in the structure of the citric acid, the oxygen containing functional groups are present after pyrolysis reaction [38].

The surface microstructure of prepared GQDs was studied by atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. 2d). The height profile of the GQDs has been shown along the line drawn in AFM image. The image shows that the height of GQDs is mainly distributed between 0.4 and 1.8 nm suggesting that they consist of more than one graphene layer [39]. Consequently, on the basis of XRD and AFM analyses, the obtained GQDs contain more than one graphene layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimating the Optical and Electrochemical Band Gap of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Pristine graphene exhibits a zero band gap and as a result the observation of its fluorescence is unlikely [40,41]. However, the size limitation in GQDs with respect to graphene results in quantum confinement and observation of larger band gap which is highly size-dependent [42].
The optical and electrochemical band gaps of obtained GQDs were measured with Tauc method and cyclic voltammetry (CV), respectively.

First, the UV–Vis spectrum of the GQDs was recorded to derive the optical band gap (Fig. 3 ). The UV–Vis spectrum shows two main peaks at 240 and 360 nm due to the π → π* transitions of graphitic CC domains and n → π* transitions attributed to the oxygen containing functional groups, respectively [43,44]. The optical band gap of GQDs was determined with using Tauc plot derived from UV–Vis spectrum. The optical band gap, Eg, and absorption coefficient, α, is related through the Tauc equation, αhυ = B(hυ − Eg)n, where hυ is the photon energy, B is a proportionality constant, and n is an exponent which equals to 1/2 for direct transitions [45]. Tauc plot in Fig. 3 shows the relation between (αhυ)2 versus hυ from which the optical band gap of the GQDs is obtained as ~4.98 eV. The observation of such band gap energy illustrates the quantum confinement effect in GQDs which increased with decreasing CC domain size [46–48].

The second approach used for determining the band gap of the obtained GQDs was cyclic voltammetry (CV) which is one of the most accurate methods for determining the HOMO/LUMO and band gap energy values of various materials [49]. Fig. 4 reveals the current-voltage curves in the absence and presence of the GQDs. The potentials were referenced versus ferrocene/ferrocenium pair as standard to determine the Eox or Ered. The calculations were done with the empirical equation ELUMO = −[(Ered − E1/2(ferrocene)) + 4.8] eV with the ferrocene value of 4.8 eV [50,51]. Based on the cyclic voltammetry results, GQDs shows Eox = 2.34 V, corresponding to EHOMO = −6.83 eV. The Ered of GQDs was determined as −2.54 V and thus the ELUMO is −1.95 eV. Based on the obtained information, the HOMO/LUMO energy level diagram of the GQDs was depicted in Fig. 5 . From the obtained EHOMO and ELUMO values, the band gap energy calculated to 4.88 eV which is close to the value determined by Tauc method (4.98 eV).

The optical properties of GQDs were also studied through the fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy. The colorless GQDs aqueous solution exhibited blue emission under excitation with UV light (Fig. 6a). As can be seen in the fluorescence spectrum, the obtained GQDs show a maximum emission at wavelength of ~460 nm indicating a Stokes shift of 100 nm with respect to the UV–Vis absorption peak centered around 360 nm.

A detailed fluorescence study was also performed with excitation of GQDs under different wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 6b, upon excitation with various wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity increased to a maximum in 360 nm and then decreased, but the position of the peaks remained constant (at 460 nm), indicating that the fluorescence origin is substantially independent of the excitation wavelength. This behavior also indicates that the obtained GQDs have uniform size and emissive sites [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the Optical Behavior of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of the GQDs were studied in acidic, neutral, and alkaline conditions and the results were given in Fig. 7 . A significant decrease in the absorption intensity of n → π* transitions can be seen under both acidic and alkaline conditions in comparison with neutral pH. Consequently, under both acidic and alkaline conditions, fluorescence intensities are lower than neutral pH. The pH-dependent fluorescence spectra show that the GQDs exhibited bright fluorescence in aqueous solution with neutral pH and the intensity of fluorescence peaks decreased by about 95% and 43% under both acidic and alkaline conditions, respectively. Thus, the neutral pH was chosen for the subsequent fluorescence studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the Fluorescence Response of the GQDs to the Methamphetamine and Morphine
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence behavior of the GQDs was studied upon addition of aqueous solutions of methamphetamine and morphine. As shown in Fig. 8 , the fluorescence intensity of GQDs solution was quenched by addition of methamphetamine while oppositely enhanced by addition of morphine. The brighter blue emission of GQDs under UV light after addition of morphine can be seen in Fig. 8. The increase in the morphine or methamphetamine concentrations gives rise to the more fluorescence enhancement or quenching of GQDs, respectively. However, unlike the obvious changes in fluorescence spectra, a slight decrease was observed in the UV absorption spectra by increasing the amount of methamphetamine and morphine.

The dependence of fluorescence quenching on the concentration of quencher species can be correlated with the Stern-Volmer equation [53]:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] is the concentration of quencher, respectively.

The Stern-Volmer plot for quenching of GQDs fluorescence in the presence of methamphetamine was shown in Fig. 8. The linearity of the Stern-Volmer plot approves the dynamic mechanism of fluorescence quenching in the presence of methamphetamine [53]. On the other hand, the plot of F/F0 against the concentration of morphine, which is inversely related to the Stern-Volmer plot, gives a similar linear plot suggesting the operation of the dynamic mechanism for the interaction between GQDs and morphine. Earlier reports demonstrated that the fluorescence properties of GQDs are affected by their surface states [54,55]. Thus, it is reasonable to say that the methamphetamine and morphine may influence on the fluorescence of GQDs by influencing on the surface states.

Comparison between the detection limit of this system and other reported methods for some of related drugs is given in Table 2 [11,56–60]. It is clear that this simply prepared sensor has a comparable detection limit and sensitivity in comparison with other complex systems which require expensive instruments and time consuming sample preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the Response Rate of the Fluorescence Signal of the GQDs to the Methamphetamine and Morphine
ce:section.ce:para: To study the response rate of the fluorescence signal of the GQDs to methamphetamine and morphine, the time-dependent fluorescence changes were monitored after addition of methamphetamine and morphine (Fig. 9 ). As shown in Fig. 9, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs at 462 nm was quenched by ~37% and enhanced by ~46% as soon as the methamphetamine and morphine was added into the GQDs solution, respectively.

In summary, GQDs were simply prepared via pyrolysis method and characterized with various physicochemical methods. The band gap of GQDs can be determined by cyclic voltammetry (electrochemical band gaps) and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy (optical band gaps). The obtained optical band gap (4.98 eV) was close to the electrochemical one (4.88 eV). The prepared GQDs were used as simple fluorescent sensor for the detection of morphine and methamphetamine in very low concentrations. The results suggest that the response of the fluorescence of GQDs to these narcotic drugs is quite rapid, implying a promising application in fast, stable, and convenient sensing of morphine or methamphetamine. The quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs could be used to develop an optical sensor for determination of other narcotic drugs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of Kharazmi University. (Grant Number D/2061).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.08.044.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
